item.title: Time for the unhinged XL Wednesday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
yeah so that is genius
bastards
order pizza whenever you want, uncle cel sez so
not sure if that's a python. maybe a ball python?
mmm hmm
aka my hero
i like how this is mostly from 1.. well, sort of
far out
torus definition. can't stand it!!!!
finally
everything is so off
not cool
stupid laundry.
we've all been there
i'm sure the scientists of Imgur will be here soon to confirm this
and balls
that's that. have a great night gang!!!

item.title: SecretHarpy's Meme Library: Volume 19
item.description:
Hello and welcome to yet another meme dump, my imgur friends. I am tired and all out of witty remarks for this one, so...onward, to memes!
Not CILANTRO MODE! I really, really hate cilantro.
Accurate and it's awful. Everything took forever as a child and now I blink and I've aged another 5 years. MAKE IT STOP
Morning people were given too much power
Just hold up I really do wanna see this lizard it looks neato HELLO LIL' LIZARD FRIEND
...yeah
Passed out drunk in a corn field somewhere, not a cell phone in sight
sus
And I love it
My dad's dead and I lost my chance to fight him. So my advice is to go fight your dad before it's too late to do so. Godspeed,
And that's all for this volume folks. Stay awesome imgur.
item.dc:creator: SecretHarpy

item.title: My Eighteenth Dump
item.description:
Today's bait image is another real pretty lady AND NOW MORE MEMES!!
(Still from the age of "everyone can buy a blue checkmark that's a cool trick" on Twitter)
I just want to say I continue to love the engagement and all the nice and funny comments! I read all of them.
Also I discovered that I called My Seventeenth Dump "My Seveteenth Dump." How's that for egg on my face.
ANYWAY LET'S GOOOOOO!!!
Oh also, there is now 883 images left in the folder, and today's dump starts at November 16th 2022.
Still waiting to get this done on needlepoint by a friend. You know, in lieu of a Live, Laugh, Love sign.
I used to do this. Leading up to Endgame I did it. The Marvels was the first MCU movie I didn't catch in cinemas.
You should probably all favourite this one. It is the best "right in front of my" meme. For the rangoons.
... I have never had a crab rangoon.
I wouldn't mind a new dishwasher. But then, I live alone.
HAHAAA GOTTEEEEEM!! *furious dabbing*
"Ringo Wants To Sing More" by CollegeHumor is pretty good. Y'all should YouTube that.
Ducking love a good 40k meme.
Ain't that the truth. Y'all excited for The Elder Scrolls VI: Psych It's Skyrim Again?
I remember this one from a previous dump of mine! One of the early ones.
And the Rear Dadmiral is found in the "suggested videos" tab on PornHub.
I absolutely went "TAKE ME TO CHURCH!!" with full volume and confidence in a car full of people, and let me tell ya, it was NOT that part of the song yet.
Probably one of the top memes enjoyed by myself and my brother. This and "fuck off ya spooky midget."
Remember? With the dildo emporium?
I'd watch it. I mean, can you imagine? I might even start painting my own just to chill out with Henry on the telly.
I don't think it'll be good, but I'm too big a fan not to be excited.
There's a longer version of this comic somewhere. Someone will post it in the comments.
Oopsie-doopsie, I muffed it up again!
Oh, I was reminded yesterday that all the pretty cosplay ladies and whatnot that I post in my dumps all have social media accounts, and sometimes are even imgur users, and I should probably credit them. The problem is, I don't really know who they are, I just have the pictures on my phone. So I really appreciate y'all giving them shoutouts in the comments! Thanks guys!
I used the fourth panel as a Discord pfp once. Again, do not have the sauce, and for that I am sorry.
Really hope the mods don't get me for this one. Unless it's for the shitty cropjob, that would be warranted.
See y'all tomorrow!
item.dc:creator: Kateness

item.title: Life as a Japanese language student - A 1 year retrospective (essay length)
item.description:
Hey, all. As the school year has ended here in Japan, I decided to think back on everything I've learned and how/why I even got here in the first place. Most of my thoughts will be accompanied by unrelated food pics that have accumulated over time, but I never felt were worth sharing by themselves. Before I get started, here's to all my Japanese friends: いつもありがとうございます。いつかまた会いたい！お疲れ様です！
1. Deciding to enroll in a Japanese language school. Back in 2018, I got the idea that I would like to visit Japan; I'd been interested in Japanese culture since a young age, and was finally at a point in my life where I was somewhat financially stable. Making several new Japanese friends that same year gave me the push to look into some travel options and what the cost would look like. I turned to Mr. Affable himself, Chris Broad, and his compatriots for advice on how to travel Japan and make the most of my time there. I ended up booking a 2 week guided tour with Inside Japan for the summer of 2019. The Tokaido Trail tour took me from Tokyo to Kamakura, Hakone, Kyoto, Osaka, and then back to Tokyo. The Tokaido Trail itself was certainly the highlight of the trip. It was an unexpectedly fascinating history lesson with details described by our tour guide that were not present on any signboard or pamphlet. Hiking up Mt. Fuji was the lowlight of the trip due to the omnipresent fog obscuring our vision, even after we made it to the peak. Outside of the tour itself, I was able to meet with some of my new friends. Thankfully, one of them was bilingual and was able to translate for the rest of us. After returning to the US, I decided to start learning Japanese so I could speak with my friends and deepen my understanding of a culture that I was more drawn to than ever. I was also planning to visit Japan again next year, but as it was now the Fall of 2019, I think we all know why that didn't work out... My initial attempts at learning Japanese were an exercise in frustration (Duolingo was beyond useless). Essentially, I spent the first year learning how to learn. After finding a bit of success with the apps WaniKani and BunPro, I noticed my speaking and listening skills weren't progressing very much. Several friends and youtube personalities all told me that immersion was the best way to improve. This sounded good to me, so I started listening to Japanese podcasts, trying (and failing) to have conversations with my friends, and writing out sentences as ways of practice. After months of very slow progression, I decided there was only one real way to level up my Japanese. This would also have the benefit of getting me back in Japan, even with the country still on lockdown. I turned once more to Mr. Affable and his video on being a Japanese language student for a day at ISI International. I submit an application through GoGoNihon, and was on my way.
2. Japanese language school My bilingual Japanese friend suggested I choose a school in Tokyo since there was English support available in most areas as a way for me to not drown immediately after arriving. Initially, I figured I'd stay for the maximum amount of time the student visa allows (2 years), but now, after 1 year, I can definitively say that was never happening. The school was fine at the beginning. Learning the basics of the language when you have nothing to build on is very difficult, and school is a great place to get that start. However, after about 6 months, I started figuring things out on my own through real-world encounters and felt like school was lagging behind my own studies. I also felt as though school was not engaging enough for the 4 hours I was spending there every day. The exercises, textbooks, and lectures were easy to comprehend but difficult to focus on. The classes I took were designed to help students pass the JLPT. They focused on kanji, vocabulary, and grammar that are on the tests and use multiple choice questions in the same manner as the JLPT. My fellow classmates were also starting to feel burnt out partway through the last quarter as well. We all frequently shared success stories with our use of Japanese outside of class; most of them were hilarious. The students with part-time jobs all claimed they learned more from a couple shifts at work than a full week of school. I'm glad I spent this past year in a JLS, but I don't think there's any way I could keep doing it.
3. Personal growth I'll keep this one short cuz I really don't like talking about myself. I feel like I've learned just as much about myself as I have about Japan this past year. Meeting dozens of new people from different parts of the world and becoming friends with them has been enlightening. I've also realized that for as much of a hermit as I've been throughout the years, I desperately need people in my life and actually find great joy in coming to know them. *edit* I have to expand on this section a bit because I can't believe I left out some of the most important aspects of my language learning here. In the final 3 months of my time here, I started practicing my Japanese outside of the classroom much more frequently. The amount of positive encounters I had were greater than the amount in the first 9 months. It's astounding how much people open up to you immediately if you demonstrate any level of Japanese comprehension. Normally I keep to myself, but I had to keep chasing that high I got every time an elderly person had words with me on the street or a new mother thanked me for being kind to her and her baby. My favorite of those two were the time I and a friend were the only customers at a restaurant before a mother and her young daughter came in. The girl kept staring at us from her chair until we made eye contact with her. At which point she greeted us with "こんにちは！". I'm certain that at no point did the thought occur to her that we were foreigners and might not speak Japanese. She continued with the common self-introduction that all students learn, and when it was our turn, we responded in kind. The mother did not hide her joy at this exchange at all. It was hard to keep my eyes on the girl as she spoke cuz the mother's beaming smile was so distracting. We waved a cheery goodbye to the little girl as we finished up our meal to head out. I had many more exchanges with children that were just as heartwarming, but that one really stuck with me. The other was with a nice elderly lady who ran a soba restaurant with a seating capacity for 3 people. Our exchanges were brief to start, but after a few visits, she was greeting me with a smile and started initiating conversation instead of waiting for me to start. We mostly spoke about her past, the shop itself, and the town. I was surprised she was excited about the Mega Don Quixote they just finished building. I expected a small business owner to be against such a thing, but she assured me that it was good for the town. If anyone has to close because of it, it'll work out in the end. She certainly was wise.
4. Japan itself Oh, man, do I ever love living in a walkable city, having health care, and not having to drive. (Did I just give away my country of origin?) Japan is not without its faults, though. There's stagnant wages, workplace harassment and overwork, severe lack of mental health support, broken homes, homelessness, and a lot of people from the 1950's still in power and kept there by their cohorts and a lack of interest in politics from younger folk. Harassment, bullying, and mental health problems are the things I see most often. Several of my Japanese friends have told me I'm the most kind-hearted person they know, and I feel as though I'm only doing what's expected of a friend (not the bare minimum, the average). I hear stories from them about how their father abuses them, their friends take advantage of them, and how their boss verbally berates them for any kind of mistake. Apparently HR departments aren't really a thing over here, so higher-ups can get away with just about anything, as long as they aren't breaking the law (or don't get caught). There's also the tradition of fathers bathing with their daughters. Sometimes up to a definitely inappropriate age (9+ years in some cases). There are currently many teen and pre-teen girls living on the streets of Tokyo because they ran away from home to escape their fathers abuse.
5. Japan itself (cont.) For as convenient as daily life in Japan is, bureaucracy and corporations are anything but. There's always a mountain of paperwork involved when attempting to do anything. Moving, for example, requires you to visit your ward office and tell them where and when you are moving and fill out the paperwork involved. It is a tedious process with a lot of questions and at the end of all of it, you still have to update anything that has your old address on it (health insurance card, residence card, etc). And moving requires you to hire a company to help with all your stuff. You are not allowed to haul it yourself. To be honest, though, Japan isn't exactly unique when it comes to this stuff, so idk what I'm on about.
6. Daily life OK, enough griping, let's talk about the cool stuff. I love that I live 30 seconds walking distance to a convenience store, laundromat, grocery store, dentist office, hospital, and several restaurants. Every other building around me is residential, and there are almost no cars that pass through our street. Things are very quiet at all hours of the day. The train station is a 4 minute walk away and has an express train that goes straight to a major station with a dozen other lines, making travel within Tokyo fast and easy. Although, I wouldn't say it's cheap. Daily commutes to school would've cost me about 5 USD every day without a commuter pass. Even with the commuter pass, it's only about a 30% discount over a 6 month period. However, trips within the stations covered by the pass are free for those 6 months, so if I use them in addition to my school commutes, it does save me more money. Tokyo is, overall, a very convenient place to live. And safe, too. I can't believe how many elementary schoolers I see walking around public spaces unaccompanied like it's nothing. They know they're not in danger. Loved seeing kids play without a worry at the local park.
7. Travel Speaking of trains and such, long-distance trips within Japan are also very easy and relaxing. Taking the shinkansen with a friend was one of the many great memories of the past year for me. We visited Kyoto and the surrounding areas for almost a week, and I can't believe how different it was compared to Tokyo. If I ever do another long-term stay, it will be outside of Tokyo so I get a fresh experience. Tangentially, I've been thinking about traveling to countries other than Japan in the coming years. I would need someone else to go with, though. I am capable of doing lots of things on my own, but I'm capable of doing anything with someone by my side. Don't know why that is. They don't even need to be holding my hand. Just having another person there gives me the strength I need.
8. Japanese language skills I initially came to Japan so I could improve my speaking and listening skills and talk to my friends. However, as most of my friends are mangaka and game developers who wish to reach a Western audience, I've had the opportunity to translate their works to English. I've found that I greatly enjoy the creative process of translating and would like to make this a source of income in the future. I have no idea how viable it will be, but I can't stomach the thought of returning to a 9 - 5 at the moment.
9. Dating in Japan After being out of the dating game for almost 13 years, I decided to jump back in a few months ago at the behest of my new Chilean friend. The app of choice: Bumble. I had more success than I would've thought. I found multiple Japanese girls and even more expatriots living in Tokyo with similar interests and life goals. I also found a comical number of girls who claimed to be "sapiosexual" in their bio. Ultimately, I found it to be too difficult to make meaningful connections with the Japanese girls I met up with. I found their stories interesting, and they were all quite lovely. But, it turns out that having shared cultural moments in your youth is something I need to really connect with someone quickly. I'm sure if I spent more time with them, the spark would appear, but my time constraints sadly didn't allow for that. I don't mind having this many new friends, though. I am still in contact with them and doing language exchange with them regularly.
10. Going forward I hate not having a plan. Been that way since childhood. Now that I'm leaving Japan, I have multiple options to choose from, and none of them are clear winners. I suppose it's better than not having a choice at all. I need stability, but also want to go on adventures. Why can't I have it both ways... Regardless of what happens, I firmly believe that Japan will have a large presence in my life, whether I live here or not.
My tiny, little room, as it was when I first arrived, and again, as I depart. Farewell, Japan! (for now)
item.dc:creator: WyrdJP

item.title: Turned 38 and my parents ask why I have no kids yet. I tell them that between work and home life, just do not have the time.
item.description: What I actually do with my time at home with my 2 cats. Also that kids are expensive and no desire to have any in the foreseeable future.

item.title: Yeah I'm tired of being alone.
item.description: But finding a soul mate takes time and patience. It's just my life has been a miserable mess. I tell myself every day not to give up. I have very bad depression. But I always workout and meditate to relieve the pain in my heart and soul. Sorry guys just doing a little venting.
